name: Mama Quilla
description: Mama Quilla, meaning "Mother Moon" or "Golden Mother," was a significant deity in the Incan pantheon, representing the moon, women, the calendar, and marriage. She was considered the sister and wife of Inti, the sun god, and together they were revered as benevolent deities providing light and order to the world. Mama Quilla was believed to control the lunar cycles, which were crucial for agricultural planning and the tracking of time. She was also seen as a protector of women, particularly during childbirth and menstruation. Her influence extended to the regulation of the Incan calendar, which was based on lunar observations. Temples dedicated to Mama Quilla were adorned with silver, reflecting the moon's silvery glow, and priestesses served her, offering prayers and sacrifices. It was believed that eclipses occurred when a beast attacked Mama Quilla, and the Inca would make noise to scare the beast away, ensuring her return. Mama Quilla symbolized beauty, tranquility, and the cyclical nature of life, embodying the feminine principle in the Incan worldview. She was a vital figure in maintaining cosmic balance and ensuring the prosperity of the Incan Empire. She was also associated with silver, considered a sacred metal. Her worship was widespread, and she was deeply integrated into the daily lives and rituals of the Incan people. Mama Quilla's influence can still be seen in the cultural traditions and beliefs of the Andean region today.
subtitle: Mother Moon, Goddess of Women

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the Moon
keyMyths.description: Mama Quilla was created alongside Inti to provide light and order. She became the moon, illuminating the night and regulating the calendar.

keyMyths.title: Mama Quilla and Eclipses
keyMyths.description: Eclipses were thought to be attacks on Mama Quilla by a celestial beast. The Inca would make noise to scare the beast away and protect her.

keyMyths.title: Protection of Women
keyMyths.description: Mama Quilla was the protector of women, especially during childbirth and menstruation. Prayers were offered to her for safe delivery and well-being.

sources.author: Maria Rostworowski de Diez Canseco

worshipPractices.name: Offerings of Silver
worshipPractices.description: Silver was closely associated with the moon due to its color and reflective properties. Offerings of silver objects, such as figurines or jewelry, were commonly made to Mama Quilla to honor her and seek her blessings.

worshipPractices.name: Sacrifices of Animals
worshipPractices.description: While human sacrifice was rare, animal sacrifices, particularly of white animals like llamas, were offered to Mama Quilla. The color white symbolized purity and was considered fitting for a lunar deity.

worshipPractices.name: Temples and Shrines
worshipPractices.description: Temples and shrines dedicated to Mama Quilla were built throughout the Inca Empire. These sacred spaces were often tended by priestesses who were responsible for conducting rituals and maintaining the shrines.

festivals.name: Coya Raymi
festivals.description: Coya Raymi was a festival dedicated to women and fertility, closely associated with Mama Quilla. It involved rituals and ceremonies to ensure successful harvests and the continuation of the Inca lineage. It was also a time for honoring the Coya, the Inca queen.

festivals.name: Inti Raymi
festivals.description: While primarily dedicated to Inti, the sun god, Inti Raymi also acknowledged Mama Quilla's role in the cosmic balance. Celebrations included feasting, dancing, and prayers for the continued prosperity of the Inca Empire.

cultural.modernLegacy: Mama Quilla's influence persists in Andean cultures through continued respect for the moon's cycles and their impact on agriculture. Indigenous communities often incorporate lunar symbolism in their traditions and artwork, preserving her memory and significance.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Mama Quilla's origins are deeply intertwined with the Incan creation myths. As the daughter of Viracocha, the creator god, she emerged into existence alongside her brother Inti, the sun god. Their birth was essential for bringing light and order to the world, which was previously shrouded in darkness. Mama Quilla's role as the moon was to provide illumination during the night, offering a gentler, more reflective light than her brother's. She was also considered the sister-wife of Inti, forming a divine couple that represented balance and harmony within the cosmos. This familial connection emphasized the interdependence of the sun and moon in maintaining the natural order and ensuring the well-being of the Incan people. Her emergence marked a pivotal moment in the Incan cosmology, establishing the foundation for their understanding of time, seasons, and the rhythms of life.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: One prominent myth involves Mama Quilla weeping tears of silver, which fell to earth and became the source of silver deposits. This tale highlights the connection between the moon and the precious metal, solidifying silver's sacred status in Incan society. Another story tells of Mama Quilla's role in protecting women, particularly during childbirth. She was invoked for safe delivery and the well-being of newborns. Eclipses were viewed with great fear, as they were interpreted as a sign of Mama Quilla's displeasure or illness. During eclipses, the Inca would make loud noises and perform rituals to scare away whatever was harming her, ensuring her return to full strength. These myths and stories reinforced Mama Quilla's importance in the daily lives of the Inca, shaping their beliefs, rituals, and understanding of the world around them. The stories also reinforced her role as a protector and provider, solidifying her position as a revered deity.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Mama Quilla was worshipped throughout the Inca Empire, with temples and shrines dedicated to her in various locations. Priestesses, often women of high social standing, played a central role in her cult, conducting rituals and maintaining the sacred spaces. Offerings of silver, textiles, and animal sacrifices were made to appease her and seek her blessings. The lunar calendar, which was crucial for agricultural planning, was closely tied to her worship. Festivals were held in her honor, celebrating the lunar cycles and their impact on the harvest. The Inca believed that Mama Quilla had a direct influence on fertility, both of the land and of women. Prayers were offered to her for bountiful crops and healthy children. Her worship was deeply interwoven with the daily lives of the Inca, reflecting their profound respect for the natural world and their reliance on the moon's cycles for survival.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Although the Inca Empire fell to the Spanish conquistadors in the 16th century, Mama Quilla's influence has endured in the cultural traditions of the Andean region. Many indigenous communities continue to recognize the importance of the moon's cycles in agriculture and maintain a deep respect for the lunar deity. Lunar symbolism persists in traditional textiles, artwork, and ceremonies, serving as a reminder of the ancient Incan beliefs. While syncretism with Catholicism has led to some blending of religious practices, the core essence of Mama Quilla as a powerful and benevolent lunar goddess remains. Her legacy serves as a testament to the enduring strength of indigenous cultures and their ability to preserve their traditions in the face of adversity. Contemporary artists and writers also draw inspiration from Mama Quilla, reinterpreting her stories and symbolism in modern contexts, ensuring her continued relevance in the 21st century.

shortDescription: In Incan mythology, Mama Quilla is the "Mother Moon," goddess of the lunar cycle, women, and the calendar. Sister-wife of Inti, she brings light and order.
